item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is interesting, I was on the fence until the very ending earned my upvote. Love the concept of the woman being… Cursed? Stuck? Trapped wherever she is…

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I've changed the Custom Car Magazine image used and acquired permission from the seller of the magazine itself and I've changed the description of the SCP. to correspond. A screenshot of the correspondence is attached here.

http://www.scp-wiki.net/local--files/scp-4133/Permission%20for%20image%20use

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Currently, the author of this article is attempting to get in touch with the creator of the image. In the meantime, I've taken down the image.

EDIT: The image has been replaced and, with permission from the artist, the image is now cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was all right, but the last interview could be better.
Firstly, if the woman spoke to them in a dream, I highly doubt they'd be able to remember what she said word-for-word. They might, if she says those exact same words every single time, but that raises the question of why she repeats herself.

Secondly, this is a bit of a nitpick, but I doubt a researcher would thank a D-Class for their time. It's not voluntary.

Still, upvote, mostly because I'm a sucker for cognitohazards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, we might be able to do that if we use CSS to change that block of text that goes “Tags are a nice way to organize content in your Site…” to something along the lines of “don’t tag this unless you’re tag team” or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, I don't think the copyright on that picture is compatible with this project.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, your image should be localized to the article page itself, rather than linked from a sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Self-tagging prevents proper tagging? I didn't know that.

Proper self-tagging does not prevent proper tagging. If an author doesn't know the tagging system well and adds incorrect tags, the page in question won't show up on the tech team's list of untagged articles to tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This warning should be displayed any time the tag field is!

Unfortunately I do not believe we have a way to do that on Wikidot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Self-tagging prevents proper tagging? I didn't know that.

This warning should be displayed any time the tag field is!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not gonna downvote it? But this. I was expecting a twist ending and instead all I got was a rehash of stuff we already knew.

Also I'm slightly confused by Agent Sam Redacted's testimony. Does everyone develop detailed memories of the person who originally got the voodoo practitioner to create SCP-4133? That seems like an awfully specific and convenient thing to accidentally get baked into an SCP Object, and it pretty much takes all the mystery out of the skip.

I wish I could come up with a concrete suggestion on how to really knock it out of the park, because there's traces of a solid theme here. Searching obsessively for a particular half-remembered piece of porn cheesecake. What's so scary about that? Maybe the fact that behind every centerfold, there was a real person with an identity, hopes and dreams. Maybe the fact that vintage centerfolds, in particular, are relics from a bygone age. That the photo was taken long before you were even born, but your reaction to it is real and visceral in the present? That the memories you're so desperately trying to preserve or recapture are frivolous in the first place? It's like, it's not even your nostalgia, dude! Your grandpa jerked off to this. Or hung it up in a garage to prove to everyone working there how straight he was. Or whatever this pre-internet semi-porn was used for. See what I mean? I don't even have a firm grasp of what magazine centerfolds were for. It was a cultural phenomenon from before my time. Might as well be an erotic phonograph record or something.

Maybe the takeaway is, at its core, all horror is old junk from your basement or attic that thinks it's alive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, do not self-tag unless you understand the tagging system well, else the tech team will not be able to properly tag your article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m stuck between a + or -. The first half is great and has a alright story with some off sentencing and spelling. But the testing and addendums is where I think you may need to read again. IMO I believe this SCP needs to be revised.

No vote.

item.title: I’m unsure.
item.content:encoded: It’s a neat concept. But the execution was off. The sentence structure was weird. I recommend a few more revisions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be written better and the containment procedures seem highly overboard. Wouldn't the foundation be able to detect when someone looks at an image of it. Imagine how many people search "silver" daily. Otherwise, decent story, but feels a little too much like SCP 2316.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s an interesting idea. I did upvote but I would look through it and reword some of the sentences. Overall I saw a lot of good effort

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was admittedly wavering throughout, but the weak ending in the third log sealed my downvote.
